The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 156km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 87min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 199km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (65kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 19%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 44min, 3rd: 56min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 203km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (72kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (35kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 205km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (69kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (52kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 182km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 87 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 61min, 3rd: 75min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute travel time from start to destination 205 km upstream (total upstream region). The boat starts at 9:00 AM, but we need total travel time (duration), not arrival time. Need to consider speed limits, cargo weight thresholds, congestion period, rest schedule, rest points every 13 km.

We have zones: first 35 km is Zone A, with speed limit 25 km/h (effective speed after current). Then Zone B is remainder (205-35 =170 km) with speed limit 19 km/h.

Boat speed in still water = 34 km/h. Current = 6 km/h. In Zone A (upstream) effective speed = boat - current = 34 - 6 = 28 km/h raw, but limited by speed limit (effective speed cannot exceed limit). However speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we need to compute the effective speed limited to zone speed limit (maybe also reduced by cargo etc). The boat's effective raw speed is 28 km/h. Is this above the limit? Limit is 25 km/h (or reduced) so we must cap at limit. So actual effective speed = min(28, limit after reductions). If limit lower, we go at limit. Note that the limit is on effective speed, so we don't need consider current separately for speed calculation; we just use the lower of raw effective speed and limit.

Similarly in Zone B (downstream) effective raw speed = boat + current = 34+6 = 40 km/h. Limit is 19 km/h (or reduced). So will be capped at limit.

Thus actual speed is limit after adjustments (since raw > limit). So we just need to compute adjusted limits based on cargo weight and congestion.

Cargo: initial cargo: 18 units *69 kg = 1242 kg. 3 units *52 kg = 156 kg. Total = 1398 kg.

Safety threshold = 800 kg. Since exceeds, we reduce speed limits: Zone A limit reduced by 15%, Zone B reduced by 23% (pre-congestion).

Compute: Zone A base limit 25 km/h reduced 15% => 25 * (1-0.15)=21.25 km/h.

Zone B base limit 19 km/h reduced 23% => 19*(1-0.23)=19*0.77=14.63 km/h.

Now congestion period: from 10:00 to 13:00 (3 hours), during which Zone B speed limit is additionally reduced by 24% (on top of cargo regulation). So during 10-13, Zone B limit = 14.63 * (1-0.24) = 14.63 *0.76 = 11.1188 km/h.

Outside that period, Zone B limit = 14.63 km/h.

Now at 182 km waypoint (i.e., after traveling 182 km from start), unload 2 units of relief supply box (each 52 kg) = 104 kg removed. The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 77km waypoint, 10 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 20%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 59min, 3rd: 71min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 208km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 4 units of construction material (58kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 20%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 156km waypoint, 1 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need to compute travel time considering speed limits per zone, cargo weight thresholds, congestion period, rest constraints.

Boat speed in still water = 36 km/h. River current = 5 km/h.

Effective speed upstream/downstream:
- Zone A: downstream, so effective speed = boat speed + current = 36+5 = 41 km/h before limits.
- Zone B: upstream, effective speed = boat speed - current = 36-5 = 31 km/h before limits.

But there are speed limits: zone A limit 28 km/h, zone B limit 19 km/h. These limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current, i.e., the boat cannot exceed those effective speeds.

If cargo weight > 700 kg, zone A limit reduced by 23%, zone B limit reduced by 25%.

Congestion period 10:00-12:00: speed limits in all zones additionally reduced by 20% (on top of cargo reduction).

Rest: cannot operate continuously more than 91 minutes. Rest points every 12 km (designated). Base rest duration 36 minutes. Each subsequent rest adds 13 minutes.

We need total travel time including rests from start 9:00 AM to final destination (208 km). Also note at 156 km waypoint unload 1 unit of construction material (58 kg). Then cargo weight reduces; may affect speed limits after that point.

First compute cargo weight initially: 18 relief boxes *58 = 1044 kg. 4 construction material *58 = 232 kg. Total = 1276 kg. That's > 700 kg, so speed limits reduced initially.

After unloading 1 construction material at 156 km: weight reduces by 58 kg, remaining cargo weight = 1276 - 58 = 1218 kg, still > 700, so speed limits still reduced.

So cargo threshold never changes relative to >700, so speed limit reductions remain throughout. So we can treat cargo reduction applied always.

B reduced by 25% => new B = 19 * 0.75 = 14.25 km/h.

Now congestion period 10-12 reduces limits further by 20% (multiply by 0.8). So during that time, effective limits are:

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 219km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (64kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 61min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

At the 52km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need to calculate travel time from start (0 km) to 216 km upstream (destination). Boat speed in still water = 38 km/h. River current = 6 km/h. Similarly Zone B (rest after 25 km, remaining 191 km) current upstream: effective speed = boat speed - current = 32 km/h (38 - 6). Speed limit 19 km/h (effective). Cargo may reduce.

Now we need to incorporate cargo weight thresholds: safety weight threshold is 800 kg. Need to compute total cargo weight initially: 12 units medical kit each 7 kg: 12*7=84 kg. Relief supply boxes: 6 units *34 = 204 kg. Total initial cargo weight = 84+204 = 288 kg. That's well below 800 kg, so cargo does NOT exceed threshold. So no reduction? Wait: Safety weight threshold 800kg, cargo exceeds it -> reduction. Here cargo is 288 kg, below, so no reduction. So speed limits remain 32 km/h Zone A, 19 km/h Zone B (subject to congestion period reduction later). At waypoint 52 km (which is after Zone B start), unload 3 units of relief supply box (each 34 kg) => reduce weight by 3*34=102 kg. After unloading, cargo weight becomes 288-102=186 kg, still below threshold, so no speed limit reduction.

Thus speed limits unchanged except congestion period: from 9:00 to 12:00, speed limit in Zone B reduced by additional 20%, applied on top of cargo regulation (none). So effective speed limit during 9-12 for Zone B = 19 km/h * (1-0.20) = 15.2 km/h. Outside that time, it's 19 km/h.

But we also have the effect of current: speed limit is on effective speed (including current). So we must ensure actual effective speed does not exceed limit. The boat can travel up to its maximum effective speed given by its own speed relative to water plus/minus current, but limited by speed limit. So in Zone A, maximum effective speed possible = 44 km/h, but limited to 32 -> target effective speed = 32 km/h. In Zone B, effective speed without limit = 32 km/h, but limit 19 km/h (or 15.2 during 9-12). So actual effective speed = 19 km/h (or 15.2). Therefore boat must throttle down.

Now we have rest constraints: cannot operate continuously > 82 minutes (i.e., must stop after max 82 minutes of travel). Rest only at designated rest points every 13 km. So there are rest points at distances: 13 km, 26 km, 39 km, 52 km, 65 km, 78 km, 91 km, 104 km, 117 km, 130 km, 143 km, 156 km, 169 km, 182 km, 195 km, 208 km, 221 km (beyond destination). We can also potentially skip a rest point if the time between last rest and next rest (or to destination) is <= 82 min, because rest only permitted at designated points, but not required? Problem says "Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km)." It doesn't say mandatory at each point. So we can choose to rest at some points if needed due to max continuous operation limit. Also we need to consider that after any rest, cumulative fatigue makes next rest longer.

Goal: compute total travel time including mandatory rests to reach final destination, obeying max 82 min continuous operation.

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 228km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 65km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need compute travel time including rests. Steps:

- River total distance = 228 km upstream? Actually upstream region spanning total 228km; meaning from start to destination 228 km upstream. We have zones: first 30km Zone A (downstream current effectively adds speed), then Zone B rest of distance? After 30km, rest is upstream (so effective speed = boat speed - current). But total includes both zones. Is there any further zone? Probably after Zone B rest of distance = 228-30 = 198 km is Zone B.

- Boat speed in still water = 41 km/h. Current =5 km/h.

Effective speed in Zone A (downstream) = 41+5 = 46 km/h. But speed limit applies to effective speed. The zone A speed limit is 30 km/h (unless cargo >600 reduces). So actual speed in Zone A is min(effective speed, speed limit). Since limit lower than effective possible, boat must go at limit, i.e., 30 km/h effective downstream speed. The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 8 units of construction material (65kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 143km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Given boat speed in still water 30 km/h. River current 6 km/h. But there is zone speed limit: 23 km/h (effective speed limit). However cargo weight may reduce limit. Also congestion period 9-1 reduces further.

We need to break path into segments: zones, rest points every 11 km (i.e., at 11km,22km,33km,... up to maybe 187?). However cannot rest continuously more than 88 minutes - meaning the boat cannot operate continuously >88 minutes without rest. So after traveling for up to 88 minutes, must rest at next rest point? Must rest only at designated rest points every 11 km. So we need to schedule rests ensuring that travel segments between rests do not exceed 88 minutes of operation (i.e., travel time). We must compute travel speed per segment based on effective speed (taking into account speed limits, cargo, congestion). Need to apply cargo regulation: if total cargo weight >600 kg, then speed limits reduced: Zone A limit reduced by 16%, Zone B by 27%. Starting cargo: 10 units relief box 54kg each =540 kg, plus 8 units construction 65kg each =520 kg. Total =1060 kg >600 kg. So cargo weight exceeds threshold. So speed limits get reduced.

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we need to compute adjusted limits.

First compute base speed limits: Zone A: 23 km/h, Zone B: 19 km/h.

Cargo reduction: Zone A limit * (1 - 0.16) = 23 * 0.84 = 19.32 km/h. Zone B limit * (1 - 0.27) = 19 * 0.73 = 13.87 km/h.

Now congestion period from 9:00 to 13:00 reduces limits further by 22% (multiplies by 0.78). So during that time, limits become:

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 239km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (74kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 19%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 78km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 63min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 19%.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 227km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 8 units of construction material (56kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 117km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (69kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (56kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 84km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 68min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 3 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 20%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 130km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 73min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Effective speed in Zone A (upstream) = boat speed - current = 38 - 5 = 33 km/h. But speed limit is 27 km/h. So must limit to 27 km/h effective speed (i.e., actual speed relative to ground). So effective is limited by limit.

Zone B effective speed (downstream) = boat speed + current = 38 + 5 = 43 km/h max, but limit 19 km/h effective. That's low; likely need throttle to keep effective speed at limit.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 22km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 180km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 221km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (79kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (59kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 84km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 79min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute travel time with variable speeds, rest stops, congestion period reductions, cargo weight changes.

Let's outline.

Total distance: 221 km. Zones: first 26 km Zone A, then rest (221-26=195 km) Zone B.

Speed limits are given as effective speed after accounting for current.

First, compute boat speed in still water: 39 km/h. Current 5 km/h.

In Zone A (downstream), effective speed = boat + current = 44 km/h (max possible) but limited by speed limit.

In Zone B (upstream), effective speed = boat - current = 34 km/h, limited by speed limit.

But speed limits are given for effective speed: Zone A limit 31 km/h, Zone B limit 19 km/h. So actual speed = min(effective speed without limit, limit after adjustments). Since effective speed capability (44,34) exceeds limits, we use the limits.

Now cargo weight impacts limit reductions.

Cargo initial: 16 units *79kg = 1264 kg. 5 units *59kg = 295 kg. Total = 1559 kg. Exceeds 1000kg, so speed limits reduced: Zone A reduced by 18%, Zone B by 23%.

Compute reduced limits:

Zone A base limit 31 km/h. Reduce 18% => multiply by (1-0.18)=0.82. 31 *0.82 = 25.42 km/h.

Zone B base limit 19 km/h. Reduce 23% => *0.77 = 14.63 km/h.

Now congestion period 8:00-11:00: additional reduction of 25% applied on top of cargo-regulated limit.

So during that time, effective speed limit = prior limit *0.75.

Thus during 8-11, Zone A limit = 25.42 *0.75 = 19.065 km/h. Zone B limit = 14.63 *0.75 = 10.9725 km/h.

Outside congestion (i.e., before 8:00 and after 11:00) limit = cargo-regulated only: 25.42 km/h in A, 14.63 km/h in B.

We also have rest points every 12 km. Must rest after traveling max continuous 92 minutes (i.e., 1h32m). If the next rest point is reached earlier, you can rest there; can't exceed 92 minutes without rest. Rest can only be at designated points (every 12 km). So we need to schedule rests along route at multiples of 12 km: 12,24,36,... up to <=221.

We need to compute travel segments between rest points, accounting for varying speed limits (zone changes at 26 km, and cargo unload at 84 km). Also after unload, cargo weight reduces, potentially eliminating safety threshold, adjusting limits.

We also need to consider that after unloading at 84 km, we recalc cargo weight: we unload 5 units of construction material (each 79 kg) => weight reduction 5*79=395 kg. New cargo weight = 1559 - 395 = 1164 kg. Still exceeds 1000 kg, so speed limits still reduced (same percentages). So no change. Wait but maybe after later unload? only this unload. So limits stay reduced.

Thus cargo regulation remains throughout.

Thus speed limits are constant (with congestion reduction time windows). No further changes.

Now compute travel time ignoring rests, accounting for speed limits and time of day for segments overlapping congestion.

Then add rests, ensuring that continuous travel time between rests does not exceed 92 minutes. Since we can only rest at multiples of 12 km. We need to compute how many rests are required and total rest duration (with increasing times).

Approach: Simulate movement along distance, keeping track of time, speed limit dependent on zone and whether time is within congestion.

Let’s compute distances and speeds.

Segment 1: from 0 to 12 km (rest point 12). Within Zone A (0-26). Speed limit depends on time (maybe before 8?). Start time 7:00. Travel speed = zone A limit (cargo reduced) = 25.42 km/h (since before 8). So travel time for 12 km = distance / speed = 12 / 25.42 hours = 0.4725 h = 28.35 minutes.

Check if 28.35 mins < 92 mins, so fine. Arrive at 7:28.35 (~7:28 and 21 sec). Actually 0.35 min = 21 sec.

We'll keep minutes with fractions.

Now need to decide rest schedule. Could rest after 12 km. Since we can continue up to 92 mins, we could skip rest. But rests are mandatory only after 92 mins of continuous operation. So we could keep going until we reach either 92 mins or end of route, but rest only allowed at designated points. So we should continue until either we exceed 92 minutes or we hit a point where we cannot continue due to max continuous time crossing 92 mins before next rest point.

Thus we need to compute cumulative travel time since last rest.

Let's simulate.

Travel 12 km, takes 28.35 min. Cumulative = 28.35.

Time = same 28.35 min. Cumulative = 56.7 min.

Time for 2 km = 2 / 25.42 h = 0.0787 h = 4.72 min.

Now at 26 km, entering Zone B. Speed limit later: 14.63 km/h (cargo-reduced) unless during congestion (after 8). Let's compute travel from 26 km onward.

We need to consider congestion period start at 8:00. So far we are at 7:61.42 min = 8:01.42? Wait 7:00 + 61.42 min = 8:01.42 (since 60 min = 8:00). So we cross 8:00 during travel? question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (68kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 187km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 67min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We must compute travel time for 209 km upstream (overall). Boat speed in still water 36 km/h, current 5 km/h.

Effective speed depends on zone direction: Zone A upstream (so effective speed = boat speed - current = 36-5 = 31 km/h). Zone B downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current = 36+5 = 41 km/h).

But there are speed limits: Zone A limit 26 km/h (effective speed limit after accounting for current). Zone B limit 19 km/h.

If cargo weight >900 kg, speed limits reduced: Zone A limit * (1-0.15) = 26*0.85 = 22.1 km/h. Zone B limit * (1-0.24) = 19*0.76 = 14.44 km/h.

Check cargo weight: 13 units *68 = 884 kg. 3 units medical kit *6 = 18 kg. Total = 902 kg. That's >900 kg, so cargo regulation applies at start.

Thus speed limits: Zone A = 22.1 km/h effective speed max. Zone B = 14.44 km/h effective speed max.

Now also congestion period: from 10:00 to 12:00 (2 hours) additional reduction of Zone B speed limit by 21% applied on top of cargo adjustment. So during that time, Zone B limit = 14.44 * (1-0.21) = 14.44 * 0.79 = about 11.4076 km/h.

Now we need to compute travel with possible variable speed due to congestion period overlapping Zone B travel.

Also rest stops: boat cannot operate continuously >91 minutes (1h31m). Must rest at designated points every 11km. Rest points located at distances multiples of 11 km? Starting from 0, there are rest points at 11km,22km,33km,... Must rest there if needed but only when reaching them? We must schedule rests such that no continuous operation exceeds 91 minutes. Base rest duration 37 minutes, each subsequent rest adds 15 minutes: i.e., first rest 37, second 52, third 67, fourth 82, etc.

We need to compute total travel time including mandatory rests, following all rules.

Important: cannot operate more than 91 minutes, so after max 91 minutes of travel, must rest at next rest point (which occurs every 11km). question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 226km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (68kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 8 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 182km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 69min, 3rd: 84min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 207km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (74kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (37kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 143km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 90 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
